text: The video shows the stomach with a bougie tube inserted by anesthesia, used to suction and decompress the stomach.
The video shows the anatomical junction where the esophagus enters the stomach, providing a clear view of these structures.
The surgeon uses instruments to take down adhesions from the diaphragm, providing a clearer view of the anatomy to evaluate for a hiatal hernia.
The spleen is visible on the top right of the screen, while a special retractor lifts a small portion of the liver up and over the stomach on the top left.
The surgeon completes the dissection around the diaphragm and esophagus, confirming the absence of a hiatal hernia, and prepares to proceed with the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, a robotic tool, to cauterize and cut tissue, effectively preventing bleeding during the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon measures the gastric pouch, which is approximately two inches in size. A tunnel is created underneath the stomach to align the staplers for forming a small pouch.
The surgeon places the stapler across the stomach after creating a tunnel, measures the pouch size one last time to ensure it is small, and then fires the stapler to create the gastric pouch.

The surgeon extends the tunnel up to the top of the stomach near the esophagus. Staplers are used to divide and cut the tissue, visibly separating the newly formed gastric pouch from the gastric remnant.

The surgeon demonstrates the separation of the gastric pouch from the gastric remnant, highlighting that no part of the stomach is removed during the gastric bypass procedure, with all stomach tissue remaining in the abdomen.
After creating the gastric pouch, the surgeon brings up a Roux limb of the small intestine to connect it to the pouch. The anastomosis is performed using sutures, without the use of staplers.
The surgeon threads a suture through the corner of the gastric pouch, preparing to connect it to the small intestine to form the initial segment of the Roux limb.
The surgeon performs a gastrojejunostomy, connecting the gastric pouch to the jejunum, the middle portion of the small intestine, completing the anastomosis known as GJ.

The surgeon uses scissors to create a gastrostomy, an opening in the gastric pouch. A plastic tube, known as a bougie, is used to measure the pouch size. Subsequently, another opening is made in the small intestine, preparing for the anastomosis between the pouch and the intestine.

The surgeon completes the first layer of the gastrojejunostomy anastomosis and proceeds to suture a second layer over it, reinforcing the connection between the gastric pouch and the jejunum to prevent leaks.
The surgeon cuts the suture, revealing the final view of the roux limb of the small intestine, which is clearly visualized in the surgical field.

The surgeon divides a portion of the intestine to create the biliopancreatic limb, visible on the right side of the screen, which will facilitate the delivery of pancreatic enzymes and bile to the roux limb.
The surgeon measures and counts the length of the roux limb, confirming that the patient will have 150 centimeters of small intestine in the roux limb.
The small bowel is visualized, showing undigested food passing through until it is connected to the biliopancreatic limb, where pancreatic enzymes and bile will mix with the food.

The surgeon creates a second anastomosis by making an incision in both the roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb, then staples them together to form a jejuno-jejunostomy (JJ). This connection allows bile and pancreatic enzymes to mix with food in the roux limb, establishing a common channel for digestion.
The surgeon sutures the common enterotomy, closing the opening in the small intestine, and then uses a stapler to seal off the jejuno-jejunostomy (JJ) connection.

The surgeon closes the potential hernia space created by the connection of the two small intestines using permanent sutures, visibly sealing the hole to prevent any sliding of the intestine through the defect.

The surgeon identifies the Peterson's defect, a potential space for internal hernia, and places sutures in the mesentery of the roux limb and the colon, which is lifted to the left of the screen, to prevent future hernias.

The surgeon performs a leak test by filling the abdominal cavity with saline and instilling air into the surgical connection, observing for air bubbles in the saline. No air bubbles are detected. The saline and air are then removed, and the bougie is extracted from the gastric pouch.
The final view shows the roux limb of the small intestine with the jejuno-jejunostomy (JJ) visible on the right side of the screen.
